reasoning:
The threshold for the outcome is 9 or more protests in a 30-day window, given the historical 360-day average of ~8.583 protests per 30 days. Protest event metrics in datasets like ACLED are almost always highly over-dispersed—they occur in short, intense, multi-location waves. This implies the median monthly count is significantly lower than the mean, and most typical 30-day periods fall below the threshold unless a specific wave occurs. In 2025, there were substantial protests regarding raising the retirement age (e.g., June and November) that likely inflated the annual average. 

For the first resolution date (Jan 25, 2026), the relevant 30-day window spans Dec 26, 2025, to Jan 24, 2026. With 23 days already passed, targeted searches in local Mauritius media and English/French regional news reveal no significant mention of ongoing protests. Thus, it is extremely unlikely that the target of 9+ protests will be reached with only a few days left.

For the second resolution date (Feb 17, 2026), the 30-day window evaluates Jan 18 to Feb 16. Without visible indications of an imminent protest movement, we revert to the base-rate chance of a random month exceeding the annual mean, which is approximately 15-25%. I assign a probability of 20%, reflecting the typical characteristics of such over-dispersed distributions.
